subtopics.title: The Concept of Tawhid
common_misconceptions: Tawhid only means Allah is one
Understanding Allah's attributes means comparing Him to creation
subtopics.teaching_note: Tawhid = foundation of Islam. Three categories: 1) Tawhid ar-Rububiyyah - Allah is the sole Creator, Provider, Sustainer. 2) Tawhid al-Uluhiyyah - Allah alone deserves worship. 3) Tawhid al-Asma wa as-Sifat - Allah has unique names and attributes. Shirk (associating partners with Allah) is the gravest sin. Quranic evidence: Surah Ikhlas (112:1-4), Surah Al-Baqarah 2:163. Nigerian context: Islam practiced across ethnic groups - Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, Nupe, Kanuri.

subtopics.title: Revelation and Compilation of the Quran
common_misconceptions: Prophet Muhammad wrote the Quran (he was illiterate)
The Quran was compiled during the Prophet's lifetime
Makki and Madani refer only to place of revelation
subtopics.teaching_note: First revelation: Surah Al-Alaq (96:1-5) in Cave of Hira. Angel Jibril brought revelation. Prophet was unlettered (ummi). Compilation: written on various materials during Prophet's time. First official compilation under Abu Bakr after Battle of Yamama (many huffaz died). Standard codex under Uthman ibn Affan (burned other variants to prevent disputes). 114 surahs, 6236 verses. Makki surahs: revealed before Hijra (focus on tawhid, akhirah). Madani surahs: revealed after Hijra (focus on legislation, jihad).

subtopics.title: Science of Hadith
common_misconceptions: Hadith and Sunnah are exactly the same (Sunnah is broader)
Hadith are not necessary if one has the Quran
subtopics.teaching_note: Hadith = reported saying/action/approval of Prophet Muhammad. Isnad = chain of narrators. Matn = text. Classification by authenticity: Sahih (authentic, sound chain), Hasan (good), Da'if (weak), Mawdu (fabricated). Six authentic books (Kutub al-Sittah): Sahih Bukhari (most authentic), Sahih Muslim, Sunan Abu Dawud, Sunan at-Tirmidhi, Sunan an-Nasai, Sunan Ibn Majah. Science of hadith: scholars scrutinized each narrator's character, memory, continuity.

subtopics.title: Five Pillars of Islam
common_misconceptions: Fasting is only about abstaining from food
subtopics.teaching_note: Five Pillars: 1) Shahadah - testimony of faith ('La ilaha illallah, Muhammadur rasulullah'). 2) Salat - five daily prayers (Fajr, Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib, Isha). 3) Zakat - 2.5% of wealth given annually to poor. 4) Sawm - fasting in Ramadan (dawn to sunset, no food/drink/marital relations). 5) Hajj - pilgrimage to Makkah (once in lifetime if able). Nigerian context: Eid-el-Fitr (end of Ramadan) and Eid-el-Kabir (10th Dhul Hijjah) are national public holidays. Islamic societies in secondary schools organize jumat services and Islamic vacation courses (IVC).

subtopics.title: Biography of Prophet Muhammad (SAW)
common_misconceptions: The Prophet was born on 12th Rabi' al-Awwal (most widely accepted but debated)
subtopics.teaching_note: Born: 570 CE in Mecca (Year of the Elephant). Orphaned, raised by grandfather then uncle Abu Talib. First wife: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (proposed to him, 15 years older). First revelation: 610 CE at age 40. Hijrah to Medina: 622 CE (Year 1 of Islamic calendar). Constitution of Medina: first written constitution, established rights of Muslims, Jews, and other tribes. Major battles: Badr (victory), Uhud (setback), Khandaq (trench). Conquest of Mecca: 630 CE (peaceful, general amnesty). Farewell Pilgrimage: 632 CE, delivered Farewell Sermon. Died: 632 CE in Medina at age 63.
